code: #load the library library(RcLoggers)  #run the function (basic) data_extract <- logger_extract(  Years = 2025,   Loggers = "BUR2" ) #run the function (detailed) data_extract <- logger_extract(  Years = c(2024, 2025), #you can dowload more than one year  Loggers = c("BUR1", "BUR2"), #you can download more than one logger  Indicators = "Chlorophyll", # if you only want one indicator you can define that (it defaults to chlorophyll and turbidity)  FilterFlags = TRUE, #filtering occurs by default, however you can customise or stop filtering  FlagTags = c(1,2), #by default flags 1 and 2 are kept, you can adjust this as needed  Aggregate = TRUE, #you can aggregate data  AggregationType = "Hourly" #if you do decide to aggregate, you need to define the type of aggregation (hourly or daily)  SmallTables = TRUE #you can request that outputs are broken into several small tables   RowCount = 1500 #if you do want to break up tables, you can define the number of rows per table ) #save purrr::walk2(   data_extract,    names(data_extract),    ~readr::write_csv(.x, paste0(.y, ".csv"))   ) purrr::walk2(data_extract, names(data_extract), ~{    #create a folder name based on the first two elements of the dataframe name   folder_name <- paste(     stringr::str_split(.y, pattern = "_")[[1]][1:2],      collapse = "_")    #if you want to expand/move where the folder is located, do that here:   # folder_name <- paste0("path_to_location/", folder_name)    #if the folder doesnt already exist, create it   if (!dir.exists(folder_name)){dir.create(folder_name, recursive = TRUE)}    #save the dataframe inside the associated folder   readr::write_csv(.x, paste0(folder_name, "/", .y, ".csv"))    } )

text: general workflow functions can demonstrated follows: Take defined/loaded/calculated dataframe one: First run value_to_score() function. function takes several inputs depending type value way wish score . first example, scoring using freshwater water quality method, therefore required supply large amount information: function returns original table additional column added. column inherits name value column provided, plus string “Score” . example value column named “MyValue”, new column named “MyValueScore”. Next, required (HWP ), weight scores. acheived equal length column dataframe contains appropriate weightings score. continue use dataframe , add example weightings. Next score_to_grade function can run. example continue using dataframe . However, function can take number score columns inputs return number new columns associated text grade. score grade function extremely simple, cases just return letter grades E scores 100 0. Currently function provide grades adhere following range: 81 100 = 61 80 = B 41 60 = C 21 40 = D 0 20 = E column(s) inherits name score column provided, plus string “Grade” . , string “Score” detected, replaced “Grade”. example value column named “MyValue”, new column named “MyValueGrade”, column named “MyValueScore” new column named “MyValueGrade” Finally, table scored potentially graded can saved using save_n3_table() function. function unique apply excel native conditional formatting outputs function. .e., can save dataframe scores, automatically apply required colour scaling (red green):
code: library(RcTools)  #create an example dataframe df <- data.frame(   WQIndicator = c(rep("DIN", 3), rep("Low DO", 3)),   WQObjective = c(rep(0.02, 3), rep(90, 3)),   WQScalingFactor = c(rep(0.38, 3), rep(70, 3)),   WQValue = c(0.002, 0.017, 0.029, 65, 93, 101) )  #calculate the twentieth and eightieth percentile values df <- df |>    dplyr::mutate(     WQEightieth = quantile(WQValue, probs = 0.8),     WQTwentieth = quantile(WQValue, probs = 0.2)   ) #run the scoring function df <- df |>    value_to_score(     value = WQValue,     value_type = "Water Quality",     water_type = "Freshwater",     indicator = WQIndicator,     wqo = WQObjective,     sf = WQScalingFactor,     eightieth = WQEightieth,     twentieth = WQTwentieth   ) #create a weight column df <- df |> dplyr::mutate(Weight = c(0.1, 0.4, 0.2, 0.05, 0.05, 0.2))  df <- df |>    weight_score(score = WQValueScore, weighting = Weight) #run the function on our example data df <- score_to_grade(df, WQValueScore) #if you wanted to get grades for multiple columns this can be achieved as follows x <- score_to_grade(x, c(ScoreA, ScoreB, ScoreC)) #save the table save_n3_table(   df = df,   file_name = "my_df",   target_columns = "WQValueScore",   scheme = "Report Card" )  #you can provide columns by index, or quoted named save_n3_table(   df = df,   file_name = "my_df",   target_columns = 8,   scheme = "Report Card" )

code: #to access this function you have to explicitly call it using the triple colon (:::), as it is not natively exported. This is because it is only supposed to be used within other eReefs functions RcTools:::ereefs_input_selection() #query date range of the model. Note that if no model is provided, you are redirected to first choose a model ereefs_get_date_range("https://dapds00.nci.org.au/thredds/dodsC/fx3/gbr4_bgc_GBR4_H2p0_B3p1_Cfur_Dnrt.ncml") #put arguments into objects all_variables <- c("Turbidity", "Chl_a_sum") all_start_dates <- "2022-03-01" all_end_dates <- "2022-03-03"  #use expand grid to build a simple table of inputs all_arguments <- expand.grid(all_variables, all_start_dates, all_end_dates, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)  #map over each argument, this returns a list of data extracts multiple_extraction <- purrr::pmap(   all_arguments,     function(Var1, Var2, Var3){     extract_ereefs(       Model = "https://dapds00.nci.org.au/thredds/dodsC/fx3/gbr4_bgc_GBR4_H2p0_B3p1_Cfur_Dnrt.ncml",       Region = sf_obj, StartDate = Var2, EndDate = Var3, Variable = Var1)     }   )  #alternatively, you can manually build the table if you want more control (note that two different time spans are requested) all_arguments_2 <- data.frame(   "AllVariables" = c("Turbidity", "Chl_a_sum", "Turbidity", "Chl_a_sum"),   "AllStartDates" = c("2022-01-01", "2022-01-01", "2023-01-01", "2023-01-01"),   "AllEndDates" = c("2022-01-05", "2022-01-05", "2023-01-05", "2023-01-05") )  #you can then reproject with the custom function using another map function multiple_extraction <- purrr::map(multiple_extraction, ereefs_reproject)

Defaults NULL use_bbox_2 Boolean. bounding box sf object used (TRUE) actual outline sf object (FALSE). code: polygons <- list(   rbind(c(0,0), c(1,0), c(1,1), c(0,1), c(0,0)),    rbind(c(1,0), c(2,0), c(2,1), c(1,1), c(1,0)),   rbind(c(0,1), c(1,1), c(1,2), c(0,2), c(0,1)),   rbind(c(1,1), c(2,1), c(2,2), c(1,2), c(1,1)))  polygons <- sf::st_sfc(lapply(polygons, function(x) sf::st_polygon(list(x))))  poly_sf <- dplyr::bind_rows(   sf::st_sf(Year = 2000, Id = c("a", "b", "c", "d"), geometry = polygons, crs = 4326),    sf::st_sf(Year = 2020, Id = rep("a",4), geometry = polygons, crs = 4326))  change <- sf_change_over_time(poly_sf, "Id", 2000, 2020) #> Warning: x is already of type POLYGON. #> Joining with `by = join_by(PreValue)` #> Joining with `by = join_by(PostValue)`  tmap::tm_shape(change) + tmap::tm_polygons(fill = "ValueChange")
